elements.sent-2411-001: The product sides of the Rogers--Ramanujan identities and alike often appear to be `transparently modular' (functions).
elements.sent-2411-002: The old work by Rogers (1894) and recent work by Rosengren makes use (somewhat implicitly) of this fact for proving the identities with the help of underlying modular equations\,---\,the main challenge is verifying the latter for the sum sides.
elements.sent-2411-003: Here we speculate on the potentials of using the asymptotics of such $q$-identities for proving them, in particular, when a product side is `asymmetric', hence not modular.
elements.sent-2411-004: This note is inspired by several developments around the famous Rogers--Ramanujan identities including the old memoir \cite{Ro94 of L.\,J.~Rogers, which made an influence on the present title.}
elements.sent-2411-005: It would be also not mistaken to acknowledge that my personal interest in, taste for and education on $q$-series and Rogers--Ramanujan identities (in particular) were tremendously influenced by the work of Mourad Ismail.
elements.sent-2411-006: Particular examples of his fine work on the latter subject, again through my personal views, are \cite{GIS99,IS06,IZ18 .}
elements.sent-2411-007: I find it appropriate to dedicate this piece to Mourad on the occasion of his round birthday.
elements.sent-2411-008: To prepare the stage, we list the standard $q$-notation (when $q$ is viewed as a complex parameter we assume it to satisfy $|q|<1$): the $q$-Pochhammer symbol is given by \begin{equation}(a;q)_k=\prod_{j=0}^{k-1}(1-aq^j)\end{equation} and its multiple version is $(a_1,\dots,a_s;q)_k=(a_1;q)_k\dotsb(a_s;q)_k$.
elements.sent-2411-009: These are used below for $k$ a non-negative integer as well as for $k=\infty$.
elements.sent-2411-010: One proof of the celebrated Rogers--Ramanujan identities \begin{equation}G(q)=\sum_{k=0}^\infty\frac{q^{k^2}}{(q;q)_k} =\frac 1{(q,q^4;q^5)_\infty}, \quad H(q)=\sum_{k=0}^\infty\frac{q^{k(k+1)}}{(q;q)_k} =\frac 1{(q^2,q^3;q^5)_\infty},\end{equation} based on the original methodology of Rogers \cite{Ro94 was recently given by Rosengren~\cite{Ro24 .}}
elements.sent-2411-011: It is based on the `self-replicating' equations satisfied by (both sum and product sides of) the two functions: \begin{equation}\label{RR} \begin{aligned} G(q) =\frac{(q^8;q^8)_\infty}{(q^2;q^2)_\infty}\big(qH(-q^4)+G(q^{16})\big), H(q) =\frac{(q^8;q^8)_\infty}{(q^2;q^2)_\infty}\big(G(-q^4)+q^3H(q^{16})\big). \end{aligned}\end{equation} Similar machinery was used by Rosengren in \cite{Ro25 to prove the identities \begin{equation}A(q) =(-q;q)_\infty\sum_{k=0}^\infty\frac{q^{2k^2}}{(q^4;q^4)_k(-q;q^2)_k} =\frac{1}{(q,q^2,q^5,q^6;q^7)_\infty}, B(q) =(-q;q)_\infty\sum_{k=0}^\infty\frac{q^{2k(k+1)}}{(q^4;q^4)_k(-q;q^2)_k} =\frac{1}{(q,q^3,q^4,q^6;q^7)_\infty}, C(q) =(-q;q)_\infty\sum_{k=0}^\infty\frac{q^{2k(k+1)}}{(q^4;q^4)_k(-q;q^2)_{k+1}} =\frac{1}{(q^2,q^3,q^4,q^5;q^7)_\infty}\end{equation} by showing that \begin{equation}\label{septic} \begin{gathered} A(q)=\frac 1{(q^2;q^4)_\infty^2}\big(qC(-q^2)+A(q^8)\big), \quad B(q)=\frac 1{(q^2;q^4)_\infty^2}\big(A(-q^2)+qB(q^8)\big), C(q)=\frac 1{(q^2;q^4)_\infty^2}\big(B(-q^2)+q^3C(q^8)\big), \end{gathered}\end{equation} for both the sum and product sides.}
elements.sent-2411-012: The first thing to point out is that the product sides of all the identities above are modular functions after an appropriate normalisation (which makes identities \eqref{RR and \eqref{septic particular examples of modular equations for participating modular functions).}}
elements.sent-2411-013: Namely, for $b>a>0$ integers, the product $(q^a,q^{b-a ;q^b)_\infty$ transforms into a modular function \begin{equation}q^{bB_2(a/b)/2}(q^a,q^{b-a};q^b)_\infty,\end{equation} where $B_2(t)=t^2-t+1/6$ is the second Bernoulli polynomial; also $q^{b/24 (q^b;q^b)_\infty$ is a (weight $1/2$) modular function (a `scaled' Dedekind eta function).}}
elements.sent-2411-014: For example, if we write \begin{equation}\hat A(q)=q^{-1/42}A(q)=\frac{q^{-1/42}}{(q,q^2,q^5,q^6;q^7)_\infty}, \quad \hat B(q)=q^{5/42}B(q)=\frac{q^{5/42}}{(q,q^3,q^4,q^6;q^7)_\infty}, \hat C(q)=q^{17/42}C(q)=\frac{q^{17/42}}{(q^2,q^3,q^4,q^5;q^7)_\infty},\end{equation} then equations \eqref{septic assume a symmetric form \begin{equation}\hat A(q)=\frac{q^{1/6}}{(q^2;q^4)_\infty^2}\big(\pm\hat C(-q^2)+\hat A(q^8)\big), \quad \hat B(q)=\frac{q^{1/6}}{(q^2;q^4)_\infty^2}\big(\pm\hat A(-q^2)+\hat B(q^8)\big), \hat C(q)=\frac{q^{1/6}}{(q^2;q^4)_\infty^2}\big(\pm\hat B(-q^2)+\hat C(q^8)\big)\end{equation} (we ignore the choice of sign), in which $q^{1/6}$ corresponds to the modular normalisation of the eta-type product $1/(q^2;q^4)_\infty^2$.}
elements.formula-2411-008: \begin{equation}\hat A(q)=\frac{q^{1/6}}{(q^2;q^4)_\infty^2}\big(\pm\hat C(-q^2)+\hat A(q^8)\big), \quad \hat B(q)=\frac{q^{1/6}}{(q^2;q^4)_\infty^2}\big(\pm\hat A(-q^2)+\hat B(q^8)\big), \hat C(q)=\frac{q^{1/6}}{(q^2;q^4)_\infty^2}\big(\pm\hat B(-q^2)+\hat C(q^8)\big)\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-015: In fact, the sign variation leads to companion identities \begin{equation}A(q) =\frac{(-q,-q^5,-q^9,-q^{13};q^{14})_\infty\cdot\big(-qC(-q^2)+A(q^8)\big)}{(q^2;q^4)_\infty^2(q,q^5,q^9,q^{13};q^{14})_\infty}, B(q) =\frac{(-q,-q^3,-q^{11},-q^{13};q^{14})_\infty\cdot\big(A(-q^2)-qB(q^8)\big)}{(q^2;q^4)_\infty^2(q,q^3,q^{11},q^{13};q^{14})_\infty}, C(q) =\frac{(-q^3,-q^5,-q^9,-q^{11};q^{14})_\infty\cdot\big(B(-q^2)-q^3C(q^8)\big)}{(q^2;q^4)_\infty^2(q^3,q^5,q^9,q^{11};q^{14})_\infty}\end{equation} of \eqref{septic , and we also have similar closed forms for \begin{equation}G(q) =\frac{-qH(-q^4)+G(q^{16})}{(q^4;q^8)_\infty(q,q^9;q^{10})_\infty^2(q^6,q^{10},q^{14};q^{20})_\infty}, H(q) =\frac{G(-q^4)-q^3H(q^{16})}{(q^4;q^8)_\infty(q^3,q^7;q^{10})_\infty^2(q^2,q^{10},q^{18};q^{20})_\infty}\end{equation} complementing \eqref{RR .}}
elements.sent-2411-016: Notice however that these alternative modular equations for $A(q),B(q),C(q)$ and for $G(q),H(q)$ seem to be harder to establish directly for the sum sides of the corresponding Rogers--Ramanujan-type identities.
elements.sent-2411-017: One can also perform similar modular normalisation of the product sides of the Kanade--Russell modulo 9 conjectural identities \cite{KR15,UZ21 \begin{equation}\sum_{m,n\ge0} \frac{q^{m^2+3mn+3n^2}}{(q;q)_m(q^3;q^3)_n} \overset?=K_1(q) =\frac{1}{(q,q^3,q^6,q^8;q^9)_\infty}, \sum_{m,n\ge0} \frac{q^{m^2+3mn+3n^2+m+3n}}{(q;q)_m(q^3;q^3)_n} \overset?=K_2(q) =\frac{1}{(q^2,q^3,q^6,q^7;q^9)_\infty}, \sum_{m,n\ge0} \frac{q^{m^2+3mn+3n^2+2m+3n}}{(q;q)_m(q^3;q^3)_n} \overset?=K_3(q) =\frac{1}{(q^3,q^4,q^5,q^6;q^9)_\infty},\end{equation} to check (routinely!) that these product sides satisfy the equations \begin{equation}K_1(q) =\frac{qK_2(-q^2)+K_1(q^8)}{(q^2;q^4)_\infty(q^6;q^{12})_\infty(q^3,q^{15};q^{18})_\infty(-q^5,-q^{13};q^{18})_\infty}, K_2(q) =\frac{K_3(-q^2)+qK_2(q^8)}{(q^2;q^4)_\infty(q^6;q^{12})_\infty(q^3,q^{15};q^{18})_\infty(-q,-q^{17};q^{18})_\infty}, K_3(q) =\frac{K_1(-q^2)+q^5K_3(q^8)}{(q^2;q^4)_\infty(q^6;q^{12})_\infty(q^3,q^{15};q^{18})_\infty(-q^7,-q^{11};q^{18})_\infty}.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-019: The techniques in \cite{Ro24,Ro25 are hardly applicable to the sum sides of either identities for $K_1(q),\allowbreak K_2(q),K_3(q)$: this time we deal with double summations.}
elements.sent-2411-020: The Rogers--Ramanujan identities are first entries in a general family of the Andrews--Gordon identities, which feature for each $k\ge2$ the product sides.
elements.sent-2411-021: Similarly, the product parts of modulo 7 Andrews--Gordon identities, \begin{equation}P_i(q)=\frac{(q^i,q^{2k+1-i},q^{2k+1};q^{2k+1})_\infty}{(q;q)_\infty}, \quadwhere \; i=1,\dots,k,\end{equation} while the sum sides correspond to $(k-1)$-fold summations.
elements.sent-2411-022: For $k=3$, the eta-type products coincide with those for $A(q),B(q),C(q)$ which we have already treated in Section~\ref{sec:1 .}
elements.sent-2411-023: Similar treatment for the sum sides is not known.
elements.sent-2411-024: Unfortunately, there seems to be no simple-looking extension of the story to modulo~9,~13 (and higher).
elements.sent-2411-025: For modulo~11 and the products \begin{equation}P_i(q)=\frac{(q^i,q^{11-i},q^{11};q^{11})_\infty}{(q;q)_\infty}, \quadwhere \; i=1,\dots,5,\end{equation} we find some `surrogate' versions: \begin{equation}P_1(q^2) =\frac{P_5(-q^2)-q^6P_3(q^8)}{(q^2;q^4)_\infty^2(q^{18},q^{26};q^{44})_\infty}, P_2(q^2) =\frac{-q^2P_1(-q^2)+P_5(q^8)}{(q^2;q^4)_\infty^2(q^{14},q^{30};q^{44})_\infty}, P_3(q^2) =\frac{P_4(-q^2)-q^4P_2(q^8)}{(q^2;q^4)_\infty^2(q^{10},q^{34};q^{44})_\infty}, P_4(q^2) =\frac{P_2(-q^2)-q^6P_1(q^8)}{(q^2;q^4)_\infty^2(q^6,q^{38};q^{44})_\infty}, P_5(q^2) =q^2\,\frac{-P_3(-q^2)+P_4(q^8)}{(q^2;q^4)_\infty^2(q^2,q^{42};q^{44})_\infty};\end{equation} and there are no alternative plus-sign companions.
elements.sent-2411-026: There are more Kanade--Russell modulo 9 identities \cite{KR15,UZ21 which are not modular functions.}
elements.sent-2411-027: They include \begin{equation}\sum_{m,n\ge0} \frac{q^{m^2+3mn+3n^2+m+2n}}{(q;q)_m (q^3;q^3)_n} \overset?=K_4(q) =\frac{1}{(q^2,q^3,q^5,q^8;q^9)_\infty}, \sum_{m,n\ge0} \frac{q^{m^2+3mn+3n^2+m+4n}(1+q^{m+1})}{(q;q)_m (q^3;q^3)_n} \overset?=K_5(q) =\frac{1}{(q,q^4,q^6,q^7;q^9)_\infty},\end{equation} where the sum form of the last identity was simplified from its original by Hickerson \cite{Hi21 , who also complemented the set with the identities \begin{equation}\sum_{m,n\ge0} \frac{q^{m^2+3mn+3n^2+m+n}(1-\omega q^{m+3n+1})}{(q;q)_m(q^3;q^3)_n} \overset?=K_6(q) =\frac{(q^6;q^9)_\infty(\omega q,\ol\omega q^3;q^3)_\infty}{(q^2;q^3)_\infty}, \sum_{m,n\ge0} \frac{q^{m^2+3mn+3n^2+2n}(1-\omega q^{3m+3n+2})}{(q;q)_m(q^3;q^3)_n} \overset?=K_7(q) =\frac{(q^3;q^9)_\infty(\omega q^2,\ol\omega q^3;q^3)_\infty}{(q;q^3)_\infty},\end{equation} with $\omega$ a primitive cubic root of unity and $\ol\omega$ its conjugate, and their conjugations (so that there are four additional conjectural identities in total).}
elements.sent-2411-028: It is hard to expect any modular-type functional equations for these new instances, though the product sides may possess some modular-like behaviour.
elements.sent-2411-029: Consider a real number $a$ from the interval $0<a\le1$.
elements.sent-2411-030: In his final Example~4 in \cite{Za06 Zagier outlines the asymptotics \begin{equation}-\ln\prod_{m=0}^\infty(1-e^{-(m+a)x}) \sim\frac{\zeta(2)}{x}-\ln x^{\zeta(0,a)}+\ln\zeta'(0,a) -\sum_{n=1}^\infty\frac{B_n}{n\cdot n!}\,\frac{B_{n+1}(a)}{n+1}\,(-x)^n \nonumber =\frac{\pi^2}{6x}+\ln x^{a-\frac12}+\ln\frac{\Gamma(a)}{\sqrt{2\pi}} -\sum_{n=1}^\infty\frac{(-1)^nB_n}{n\cdot(n+1)!}\,B_{n+1}(a)x^n \label{eq:asym}\end{equation} as $x\to0$, in which $\zeta(s,a)$ denotes the Hurwitz zeta function, $B_n(t)$ and $B_n=B_n(1)$ are the Bernoulli polynomials and numbers respectively.}
elements.sent-2411-032: More generally, $B_n(1-a)=-B_n(a)$ for $n>1$ odd implying that the asymptotics \begin{equation}-\ln\prod_{m=0}^\infty(1-e^{-(m+a)x})(1-e^{-(m+1-a)x}) \sim\frac{\pi^2}{3x}+\ln\frac{\sqrt\pi}{\sqrt{2}\,\sin\pi a} -\frac12\Big(a^2-a+\frac16\Big)x+o(x^N)\end{equation} as $x\to0$, where $N>1$ can be taken arbitrary.
elements.sent-2411-033: In the case of rational $a$, writing it as $a/b$ with $0<a<b$ integers to the lowest terms and taking $2\pi bx$ for $x$, the asymptotics reads \begin{equation}-\ln(q^a,q^{b-a};q^b)_\infty\big|_{q=e^{-2\pi x}} \sim\frac{\pi}{6bx}+\ln\frac{\sqrt\pi}{\sqrt{2}\,\sin(\pi a/b)} -bB_2(a/b)\pi x+o(x^N)\end{equation} and further \begin{equation}q^{bB_2(a/b)/2}(q^a,q^{b-a};q^b)_\infty\big|_{q=e^{-2\pi x}} \sim\frac{\sqrt{2}\,\sin(\pi a/b)}{\sqrt\pi}\,e^{-\pi/(6bx)} \,\big(1+o(x^N)\big)\end{equation} as $x\to0$ for any $N>1$.
elements.sent-2411-034: This agrees with the modular behaviour (hinted in Section~\ref{sec:1 ) of the symmetric products $q^{bB_2(a/b)/2 (q^a,q^{b-a ;q^b)_\infty$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-035: At the same time the asymptotics of an individual product $(q^a;q^b)_\infty$ for $a\ne b/2$ and $a\ne b$ as $q=e^{-2\pi x}\to1$ is clearly very different, since the terms for even $n$ in \eqref{eq:asym contribute to it.}
elements.sent-2411-036: There are however (infinitely) many examples of asymmetric products compiled from $(q^a;q^b)_\infty$ that exhibit a modular-like asymptotics as $q\to1$ (but also\,---\,with a bit of extra analysis outlined in~\cite{Za06}\,---\,at $q$ approaching other roots of unity).
elements.sent-2411-037: We illustrate this on examples of the Kanade--Russell products $K_4(q)$ and $K_5(q)$.
elements.sent-2411-038: Using, for $r=3$, the summation formula \begin{equation}\sum_{k=0}^{r-1}B_{n+1}\Big(t+\frac kr\Big)=r^{-n}B_{n+1}(rt)\end{equation} for the Bernoulli polynomials and multiplication formula \begin{equation}\sum_{k=0}^{r-1}\Gamma\Bigl(t+\frac kr\Bigr) =(2\pi)^{(r-1)/2}r^{-rt+1/2}\Gamma(rt)\end{equation} for the gamma function we see that both $1/(q,q^4,q^7;q^9)_\infty|_{q=e^{-2\pi x }$ and $1/(q;q^3)_\infty|_{q=e^{-2\pi x }$ exhibit the same asymptotic behaviour \begin{equation}\frac{\pi}{36x}+\ln(6\pi x)^{-\frac16}+\ln\frac{\Gamma\big(\tfrac13\big)}{\sqrt{2\pi}} -\sum_{n=1}^\infty(-1)^n\frac{B_n}{n\cdot(n+1)!}\,B_{n+1}\big(\tfrac13\big)(6\pi x)^n \quadas\; x\to0,\end{equation} and a similar complete match takes place for the asymptotics of $1/(q^2,q^5,q^8;q^9)_\infty$ and $1/(q^2;q^3)_\infty$ as $q\to1$.}}
elements.sent-2411-039: In particular, the asymptotics of both $q^{1/12 K_4(q)$ and $q^{1/12 K_5(q)$ as $q\to1$ coincide with that of $q^{1/12 /(q,q^2;q^3)_\infty$, which is a modular function.}}}
elements.sent-2411-040: The functions $\hat K_4(q)=q^{1/12 K_4(q)$ and $\hat K_5(q)=q^{1/12 K_5(q)$ are however not modular in the classical sense but, perhaps, are components of a vector-valued modular function, hence have a controllable mock-theta-like behaviour \cite{On09,Za09 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-041: What we could check numerically is that the functions $\hat K_4(\pm q^k)$ and $\hat K_5(\pm q^k)$ for various $k$ (and a particular sign choice for each~$k$) do not seem to be linearly related to each other with coefficients from the field of modular functions.
elements.sent-2411-042: Echoing \cite{Za22 , the asymptotics at roots of unity for a particular $q$-sum-to-product identity can serve as a ground for its proof.}
elements.sent-2411-043: Though it looks like a doable task for the product side, there seem to be no efficient strategies to make it work for the sum side.
elements.sent-2411-044: One still needs a creative work in the spirit of Rogers and Rosengren \cite{Ro94,Ro24,Ro25 .}
elements.sent-2411-045: In fact, it is quite suggestive that manipulations of the type $q\mapsto\pm q^k$ are natural at the level of finite ($q$-polynomial) versions of the sum sides of Rogers--Ramanujan(-type) identities.
elements.sent-2411-046: These usually originate from combinatorial interpretations, and many are recorded in the literature; we limit ourselves by citing \cite{Si03 and \cite{UZ21 for a historical overview and references provided there.}}
elements.sent-2411-047: In spite of this personal belief there seems to be no evidence for existence of finite versions of equations \eqref{RR and \eqref{septic .. Acknowledgements.}}
elements.sent-2411-048: I am heartily thankful to Hjalmar Rosengren, Ali Uncu and Ole Warnaar for discussions on the matter.
elements.sent-2411-049: Part of the work was done during my visit in the Max-Planck Institute of Mathematics (Bonn) in summer 2023; I thank the institute for excellent working conditions provided.
elements.sent-2411-050: The old work by Rogers (1894) and recent work by Rosengren make use (somewhat implicitly) of this fact for proving the identities with the help of underlying modular equations\,---\,the main challenge is verifying the latter for the sum sides.
elements.sent-2411-051: Here we speculate on the potentials of using the asymptotics of such $q$-identities or their finite versions for proving them.
elements.sent-2411-052: They include \begin{equation}\sum_{m,n\ge0} \frac{q^{m^2+3mn+3n^2+m+2n}}{(q;q)_m (q^3;q^3)_n} \overset?=K_4(q) =\frac{1}{(q^2,q^3,q^5,q^8;q^9)_\infty} =\frac{(q^9;q^9)_\infty(\omega q^2,\ol\omega q^2;q^3)_\infty}{(q^3;q^3)_\infty}, \sum_{m,n\ge0} \frac{q^{m^2+3mn+3n^2-m+n}(1-q^{2m})}{(q;q)_m (q^3;q^3)_n} \overset?=K_5(q) =\frac{1}{(q,q^4,q^6,q^7;q^9)_\infty} =\frac{(q^9;q^9)_\infty(\omega q,\ol\omega q;q^3)_\infty}{(q^3;q^3)_\infty},\end{equation} where the sum side of the last identity was simplified from its original by Hickerson \cite{Hi21 , who also complemented the set with the identities \begin{equation}\label{prod} \begin{aligned} \sum_{m,n\ge0} \frac{q^{m^2+3mn+3n^2+m+n}(1-\omega q^{m+3n+1})}{(q;q)_m(q^3;q^3)_n} \overset?=K_6(q) =\frac{(q^6;q^9)_\infty(\omega q,\ol\omega q^3;q^3)_\infty}{(q^2;q^3)_\infty}, \sum_{m,n\ge0} \frac{q^{m^2+3mn+3n^2+2n}(1-\omega q^{3m+3n+2})}{(q;q)_m(q^3;q^3)_n} \overset?=K_7(q) =\frac{(q^3;q^9)_\infty(\omega q^2,\ol\omega q^3;q^3)_\infty}{(q;q^3)_\infty}, \end{aligned}\end{equation} with $\omega$ a primitive cubic root of unity and $\ol\omega$ its conjugate, and their conjugations (so that there are four additional conjectural identities in total).}
elements.formula-2411-027: \begin{equation}\label{prod}  \sum_{m,n\ge0} \frac{q^{m^2+3mn+3n^2+m+n}(1-\omega q^{m+3n+1})}{(q;q)_m(q^3;q^3)_n} \overset?=K_6(q) =\frac{(q^6;q^9)_\infty(\omega q,\ol\omega q^3;q^3)_\infty}{(q^2;q^3)_\infty}, \sum_{m,n\ge0} \frac{q^{m^2+3mn+3n^2+2n}(1-\omega q^{3m+3n+2})}{(q;q)_m(q^3;q^3)_n} \overset?=K_7(q) =\frac{(q^3;q^9)_\infty(\omega q^2,\ol\omega q^3;q^3)_\infty}{(q;q^3)_\infty}, \end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-053: This also explains why the functions $K_4(q)$ and $K_5(q)$, even when multiplied by a rational power of~$q$, are not modular (but, perhaps, are components of a vector-valued modular function with a controllable mock-theta-like behaviour \cite{On09,Za09 at roots of unity).}
elements.sent-2411-054: What we could check numerically was that the functions $K_4(\pm q^k)$ and $K_5(\pm q^k)$ for various $k$ (and a particular sign choice for each~$k$) do not seem to be linearly related to each other with coefficients from the field generated by $q^{k/24}$ and modular functions.
elements.sent-2411-055: At the same time the modular function $q^{1/12 K(q)$, with $K(q)=1/(q,q^2;q^3)_\infty$, alone satisfies very simple modular equations: \begin{equation}K(q)=\frac{(q^6;q^6)_\infty(q^8;q^8)_\infty^2} {(q^2;q^2)_\infty^2(q^{24};q^{24})_\infty}\,\big(K(-q^4)+qK(q^{16})\big)\end{equation} and \begin{equation}K(q)=\frac{(q^2;q^2)_\infty(q^3;q^3)_\infty^2(q^8;q^8)_\infty^2(q^{12};q^{12})_\infty} {(q;q)_\infty^2(q^4;q^4)_\infty(q^6;q^6)_\infty^2(q^{24};q^{24})_\infty}\,\big(K(-q^4)-qK(q^{16})\big);\end{equation} these resemble the tangled equations \eqref{RR for the Rogers--Ramanujan functions.}}
elements.sent-2411-056: It seems to be appropriate to comment on how one experimentally discovers the product sides of identities like \eqref{prod .}
elements.sent-2411-057: The principal point is that any (formal) power series $F(q)\in 1+q\mathbb Z[[q]]$ can be cast as the product $\prod_{k=1 ^\infty(1-q^k)^{r_k}$ with $r_k\in\mathbb Z$.}
elements.sent-2411-058: The factorisation is unique as follows from \begin{equation}\sum_{k=1}^\infty\frac{kr_k\,q^k}{1-q^k} =-q\,\frac{F'(q)}{F(q)};\end{equation} writing the latter series as $\sum_{n=1 ^\infty c_nq^n$ we deduce that $c_n=\sum_{k\mid n kr_k$, hence with the help of the M\"obius inversion formula we find out that \begin{equation}r_k=\frac1k\sum_{d\mid k}\mu\Big(\frac kd\Big)c_d\end{equation} (notice that this formula obscures the integrality of the exponents~$r_k$).}}
elements.sent-2411-059: This strategy makes it clear how the products like $K_1(q),\dots,K_5(q)$ for the sum sides of the Kanade--Russell conjectures can be found.
elements.sent-2411-060: The sums in \eqref{prod live in $1+q\mathbb Z[\omega][[q]]$; whenever we suspect a product of the type $K_6(q)$ or $K_7(q)$ for such a power series $F(q)$ we can look for one for $F(q)\ol F(q)\in1+q\mathbb Z[[q]]$ (and, in more general cases, when $F(q)\in 1+q\mathbb Z[\zeta][[q]]$ with $\zeta$ a root of unity, for the product of $F(q)$ and all its Galois conjugates) and then reconstruct the corresponding exponents for individual factors $1-\omega q^k$ and $1-\ol\omega q^k$ using the fact that the products for $F(q)$ and $\ol F(q)$ are conjugate to each other.}
elements.sent-2411-061: Since \begin{equation}K_6(q)\ol K_6(q)=\frac{(q^6;q^9)_\infty}{(q;q^3)_\infty(q^2;q^3)_\infty^2} \quadand\quad K_7(q)\ol K_7(q)=\frac{(q^3;q^9)_\infty}{(q;q^3)_\infty^2(q^2;q^3)_\infty}\end{equation} are asymmetric, neither $K_6(q)$ nor $K_7(q)$ is modular.
elements.sent-2411-062: Furthermore, \begin{equation}K_6(q)\ol K_7(q)=\frac{(q^3,q^6;q^9)_\infty(\omega q,\ol\omega q^2,\omega q^3,\ol\omega q^3;q^3)_\infty}{(q,q^2;q^3)_\infty} =\frac{(\omega q;\omega q)_\infty}{(q;q)_\infty}\end{equation} and its conjugate $\ol K_6(q)K_7(q)$ are again not modular functions.
elements.formula-2411-033: \begin{equation}K_6(q)\ol K_7(q)=\frac{(q^3,q^6;q^9)_\infty(\omega q,\ol\omega q^2,\omega q^3,\ol\omega q^3;q^3)_\infty}{(q,q^2;q^3)_\infty} =\frac{(\omega q;\omega q)_\infty}{(q;q)_\infty}\end{equation}
elements.sent-2411-063: We conclude this section echoing \cite{Za22 : the asymptotics at roots of unity for a particular $q$-sum-to-product identity can serve, at least in principle, as a ground for its proof.}
elements.sent-2411-064: Though it looks like a doable task for the logarithm of the product side, there seem to be no efficient strategies to make it work for the sum side.
elements.sent-2411-065: These usually originate from combinatorial interpretations, and many are recorded in the literature; we limit ourselves to citing \cite{Si03 and \cite{UZ21 for a historical overview and references provided there.}}
